pathophysiology_summary: A viral upper-respiratory infection (typically rhinovirus, influenza or parainfluenza) spreads to the larynx, producing inflammation and oedema of the vocal cords. The swollen, stiffened cords vibrate poorly, causing hoarseness progressing to aphonia, while laryngeal irritation drives a dry, non-productive cough. The illness is self-limiting: the acute viral phase settles over 7-10 days, though residual cord oedema can leave mild hoarseness for up to two weeks. It is benign in its natural course; the clinical task is to recognise the pattern, exclude the airway-threatening mimics (acute epiglottitis, bacterial tracheitis) by the presence or absence of red flags, avoid unnecessary antibiotics, and counsel absolute voice rest. Antibiotics are ineffective; the definitive treatment is voice rest to let the cord oedema subside.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute aphonia with a dry irritant cough on a background of three days of coryzal symptoms is the classic picture.
An airway emergency that must be actively screened for by red flags (stridor, drooling, muffled voice, severe odynophagia, high fever, tripod posture) before a benign label is accepted.
A dangerous mimic if there is high fever, toxic appearance and purulent secretions — must be excluded before reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: vocal cord structural lesion (nodule or polyp)
differential.evidence_basis: Considered for hoarseness, but the acute onset with concurrent viral symptoms points away from a chronic structural cause.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the leading benign diagnosis and the two airway mimics should all be on the list; the mimics are excluded by the red-flag screen, not assumed away.

differential.evidence_basis: Comfortable quiet breathing, no drooling, intact saliva swallow and no high fever exclude the airway emergencies and confirm the benign pattern.
Excluded by absence of stridor, drooling, dysphagia for saliva, high fever and toxaemia.
Excluded by the lack of high-grade fever, purulent sputum and lower-respiratory signs.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the red-flag screen is negative, the airway mimics move to excluded and the correct posture is self-care plus safety-netting, not escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Self-limiting viral laryngitis with an intact, patent airway; management is voice rest, hydration, simple analgesia and safety-netting.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A persistent-hoarseness pathway (>3 weeks, especially in a smoker) remains a later structural/malignancy safety-net, addressed in follow-up rather than acutely.

red_flags.description: Inspiratory stridor, difficulty breathing, drooling, or inability to swallow saliva — impending upper-airway obstruction (epiglottitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would signal an airway-threatening emergency needing immediate 000 and in-person airway management; explicitly absent in this case.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A developing epiglottitis missed on screening could progress to complete airway obstruction.

red_flags.description: High fever with toxic appearance, severe worsening throat pain, or muffled 'hot-potato' voice — bacterial complication (epiglottitis or bacterial tracheitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would move the case from benign viral illness to an urgent bacterial airway infection requiring in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated bacterial airway infection can deteriorate rapidly.

red_flags.description: Hoarseness persisting beyond 3 weeks (especially in a smoker) — structural or malignant laryngeal lesion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Persistent hoarseness mandates laryngoscopy to exclude a vocal-cord lesion or malignancy; not present acutely but a mandatory follow-up safety-net.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A delayed laryngeal malignancy diagnosis if persistent hoarseness is dismissed as ongoing viral illness.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating dramatic complete voice loss and violent retching cough as inherently dangerous and escalating to ED or 000 despite an explicitly patent airway and negative red-flag screen — over-triage of a benign self-limiting illness.
Reassuring the patient without first actively screening for stridor, drooling, dysphagia for saliva and high fever — the negatives are what make reassurance safe.
Prescribing antibiotics for a self-limiting viral laryngitis — ineffective and an error of commission.
Advising the patient to whisper to rest the voice — whispering strains the vocal cords MORE than normal phonation; the correct advice is absolute voice rest (write, do not speak or whisper).

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct visualisation of the epiglottis and supraglottis
Indirect or flexible laryngoscopy of the vocal cords (erythema/oedema without structural lesion)
Oropharyngeal examination (posterior pharyngeal wall, tonsils, uvula position, cervical nodes)
Chest auscultation and formal assessment of true airway patency
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the benign viral laryngitis pattern from the history plus an explicitly negative airway red-flag screen; provide self-care (absolute voice rest, hydration, humidification, simple analgesia) with clear airway safety-netting; do NOT refer to ED or 000 in the absence of red flags. Escalate immediately to 000 only if airway red flags develop (stridor, drooling, difficulty breathing, inability to swallow saliva).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis itself (classic pattern with negative red flags). The real risks are OVER-management (unnecessary ED referral for a benign illness) and, at the other extreme, reassuring without arming the patient with the airway safety-net.
